title: Consumption SLA Mapping Playbook: route-to-fidelity and migration guidance
preview:
Purpose and scope:

It provides a prescriptive migration and rollout plan for customers movin...
text:
Purpose and scope:

This playbook defines how to map per-route customer SLAs (latency targets, cost ceilings, and fidelity budgets) to Redwood consumption primitives (serving tiers, model variants, caching policies, and quantization profiles). It provides a prescriptive migration and rollout plan for customers moving from coarse global tiers to route- or feature-level fidelity controls.

Intended audience:
- Product managers owning consumption tiers and billable features
- Solutions and customer engineering teams executing migrations
- Backend engineers and runtime owners implementing route-level enforcement
- Finance and pricing for estimating revenue/usage impacts

High-level goals:
- Provide a repeatable mapping from customer SLA statements to Redwood feature configuration (tier, model variant, cache policy).
- Minimize customer-perceived regression risk when shifting fidelity at r...

text:
Summary:
This playbook is intended for channel and strategic partners (ISVs, systems integrators, and resellers) who are evaluating or co-selling Redwood Inference with their customers. It provides crisp positioning, a compact competitive comparison, a technical integration checklist, partner-friendly pricing scenarios for pilots and proofs-of-concept (PoCs), and a security & compliance FAQ tailored to partner conversations.

Use cases targeted:
- Embedded chat and summarization inside SaaS products (low-latency interactive path)
- High-throughput batch embeddings for search and recommendation systems
- Regulated verticals (finance, healthcare) using Private/VPC deployments

Top-level positioning (elevator + extended):
- Elevator (30s): Redwood Inference delivers predictable, low-latency LLM serving across hosted, dedicated, and private deployments so your product team can embed AI fe...

Overview

Purpose: This handbook centralizes company-wide operational controls and step-by-step workflows for access & permissions, change management, data handling, procurement & expenses, vendor management, travel, and the canonical internal templates. Its goal is to reduce ambiguity, accelerate approvals, and ensure consistent compliance with our internal risk posture and partner requirements.

Audience: All Redwood employees, contractors, and teams owning operational changes or supplier relationships.

Scope: Applies to hosted, dedicated, and private deployment controls, all production and non-production environments, and all vendors engaged on Redwood contracts.

| Access & Permissions | Security (rbac@redwood.ai) | IT Ops | Head of Se...

Overview
========

Purpose: This guide defines a single, repeatable process for performing controlled rollouts of Redwood Private deployments (VPC and on-prem) that enforces compatibility gates across runtime, model artifacts, packaging, and network/KMS dependencies. It collates pre-installation checks, staged rollout steps, day-2 validation, rollback criteria, and customer-facing readiness items so that field installs, upgrades, and hot-swap model rollouts are low-risk and observable.

Scope: applies to: 
- New environment installs (VPC private clusters and on-prem air-gapped installs)
- Versioned upgrades of the control plane and serving runtime
- Model binary swaps that change kernel/quantization profiles or KV cache format

Owners and stakeholders
-----------------------
- Primary owner: eng-private-deployments (Deployment Engineering)
- Runbook owner: on-call Private Deploys SRE ...

title: Causal Latency Attribution and Signal Enrichment Playbook
preview:
Summary and purpose

This playbook defines a repeatable approach for attributing causal sources of request latency in the serving runtime and enriching traces and metrics with synthesized signals that improve root-cause diagnosis. The intended audience is runtime engineers, SREs, and profiling/product teams who inve...
text:
Summary and purpose

This playbook defines a repeatable approach for attributing causal sources of request latency in the serving runtime and enriching traces and metrics with synthesized signals that improve root-cause diagnosis. The intended audience is runtime engineers, SREs, and profiling/product teams who investigate tail-latency incidents or validate scheduler/kernel changes against real traffic.

Goals
- Produce an actionable causal hypothesis for >80% of investigated tail-latency incidents within 48 hours.
- Ensure enriched telemetry is available for post-incident analysis and automated detection rules within 6 hours of rollout.
- Keep incremental instrumentation cost increase under 12% of telemetry budget for a standard region.

Scope and non-goals
- Scope: production serving runtime (request surface -> kernel dispatch -> GPU execution -> response), associated request routin...

title: operator-surface-modernization-roadmap

title: Observability cost-sandboxing pilot retrospective
preview:
Summary:

This retrospective covers the Observability Cost-Sandboxing Pilot (Sep 2026 — Nov 2026). The pilot tested a lightweight sandboxing workflow for experimental telemetry consumers to run new metrics/alerts in a cost-isolated environment before promoting them to production. Goals: reduce unreviewed telemetry s...
text:
Summary:

This retrospective covers the Observability Cost-Sandboxing Pilot (Sep 2026 — Nov 2026). The pilot tested a lightweight sandboxing workflow for experimental telemetry consumers to run new metrics/alerts in a cost-isolated environment before promoting them to production. Goals: reduce unreviewed telemetry sprawl, measure actual cost impact of new instrumentation, and shorten feedback loops for SLI owners.

Background:

- Problem statement:
 - Over the past 18 months we onboarded ~120 new telemetry consumers (dashboards, alerts, continuous guards). Many were created without explicit ownership or cost estimates.
 - Result: increasing ingestion bills, alert noise, and SLI signal duplication across teams.

- Pilot hypothesis:
 1) A sandboxed pathway (dev -> sandbox -> prod) with cost accounting and a lightweight review gate will reduce unnecessary production telemetry by >= 30% f...

title: SRE cascade mitigation and graded fallback runbook
preview:
h1. Purpose
This runbook defines a structured, graded approach for mitigating cascading failures in the inference stack and executing progressive fallback policies while preserving customer-facing SLOs. It unifies SLO/error-budget signals, capacity operations, routing decisions, and communication steps so on-call en...
text:
h1. Purpose
This runbook defines a structured, graded approach for mitigating cascading failures in the inference stack and executing progressive fallback policies while preserving customer-facing SLOs. It unifies SLO/error-budget signals, capacity operations, routing decisions, and communication steps so on-call engineers and SRE playbooks can perform safe, reversible actions under pressure.

h2. Scope
- Applies to: Redwood inference-serving clusters (hosted + dedicated) and the edge routing plane.
- Services covered: model-router, request-queue, batch-worker, kv-cache, gpu-proxy, autoscaler.
- Not covered: control-plane database migrations, onboarding new model artifacts.

h2. Background and motivation
Cascading degradation often begins with a localized capacity pressure or a latency spike that propagates through backpressure to upstream routers and clients. The objective of this ru...

title: Developer Fidelity Guardrail Assistant — Product Brief
preview:
Overview

This brief describes the Developer Fidelity Guardrail Assistant (DFGA), a product module in Redwood Optimize intended to help developers detect, understand, and automatically remediate fidelity regressions and cost-risk in early-stage integrations. The DFGA combines lightweight local simulation, telemetry-...
text:
Overview

This brief describes the Developer Fidelity Guardrail Assistant (DFGA), a product module in Redwood Optimize intended to help developers detect, understand, and automatically remediate fidelity regressions and cost-risk in early-stage integrations. The DFGA combines lightweight local simulation, telemetry-driven heuristics, and in-console prescriptive actions so teams can move from exploratory API usage to production-safe consumption with minimal manual tuning.

Goals

- Reduce time-to-safe-production for developer integrations by detecting fidelity vs. cost regressions during onboarding and early ramp.
- Provide actionable remediation suggestions (config changes, candidate model variants, quantization presets) scoped to a customer’s observed workload.
- Integrate with Console, SDKs, and CI pipelines: capture preflight checks, post-deploy regressions, and staged rollout gate...

title: variant-marketplace-prd-catalog-and-addon-pricing-2026-09-10
preview:
Overview:
This PRD describes the Variant Marketplace: a first-class product surface that lets customers discover, select, and meter curated model variants and optional add-ons (latency tiers, quantization profiles, distillation presets, and managed warm pools). The Marketplace enables price differentiation by varian...
text:
Overview:
This PRD describes the Variant Marketplace: a first-class product surface that lets customers discover, select, and meter curated model variants and optional add-ons (latency tiers, quantization profiles, distillation presets, and managed warm pools). The Marketplace enables price differentiation by variant and supports metered add-ons to improve unit economics without sacrificing discoverability or operational controls.

Context and motivation:
- Customers increasingly want to choose model tradeoffs (cost vs quality vs latency) without building custom model ops.
- Current catalog is binary (model version). We need a marketplace UX + billing contract that encodes variant attributes and add-on metering.
- This unlocks: explicit pricing for lower-cost quantized variants, premium pricing for ultra-low-latency variants on dedicated capacity, and per-customer curated variant bund...

title: Automated Degradation Classifier and Customer Failover Runbook
preview:
Overview

This runbook describes the automated degradation classifier (ADC) used by Customer Success and SRE to detect service-quality regressions for enterprise tenants, validate safe failover eligibility, and orchestrate tiered customer notifications and remediation. It documents classification thresholds, telemet...
text:
Overview

This runbook describes the automated degradation classifier (ADC) used by Customer Success and SRE to detect service-quality regressions for enterprise tenants, validate safe failover eligibility, and orchestrate tiered customer notifications and remediation. It documents classification thresholds, telemetry mappings, alert routing, escalation windows, and step-by-step procedures for automated and manual remediation.

Goals

- Detect real customer-impacting degradation (latency, error-rate, model-quality drift) with <80% false-positive rate in steady-state traffic.
- Automate non-destructive mitigations (routing to fallback model variants, traffic shaping) within 5 minutes of classification.
- Provide clear, auditable customer communications and playbook for escalation to engineering/ops.

Scope

- Applies to Dedicated and Private tenants and high-volume Hosted API customers...

This playbook documents a standardized approach for identifying, preventing, and remediating failures caused by third-party or internal dependency outages during enterprise onboarding and early go-live. It focuses on the technical mitigations, telemetry assertions, and communication patterns Customer Succes...
text:
Purpose

This playbook documents a standardized approach for identifying, preventing, and remediating failures caused by third-party or internal dependency outages during enterprise onboarding and early go-live. It focuses on the technical mitigations, telemetry assertions, and communication patterns Customer Success (CS), SRE, and Platform teams should follow when dependencies degrade or fail.

Scope

- Applies to enterprise onboarding windows, canary rollouts, and first-72-hour post-go-live stability runs.
- Covers external partners (cloud provider APIs, SCM, identity providers, SaaS connectors) and internal services we rely on (ingress-gateway, fanout-proxy, telemetry-gatekeeper, embedding-indexer, kvcache, model-router).
- Does NOT cover purely customer-side outages (e.g., customer DB down) except where we coordinate cutover.

Owners and RACI

title: API Telemetry and Cost-SLO Governance Playbook
preview:
Summary:
This playbook defines a company-wide standard for tying API-level telemetry to cost signals and SLO governance. It prescribes the required telemetry envelope, SLI definitions that include cost attributes (token-cost, GPU-seconds, egress bytes), sampling and retention rules, alert gates, and rollout steps fo...
text:
Summary:
This playbook defines a company-wide standard for tying API-level telemetry to cost signals and SLO governance. It prescribes the required telemetry envelope, SLI definitions that include cost attributes (token-cost, GPU-seconds, egress bytes), sampling and retention rules, alert gates, and rollout steps for adopting cost-aware SLOs across Redwood services.

Motivation:
- Inference economics are a first-class operational concern at Redwood. Teams must be able to reason about unit cost alongside latency/availability.
- Current SLOs are primarily latency/availability focused; missing cost signals lead to reactive and inconsistent capacity decisions.
- A standardized telemetry + cost tagging model enables automated budget enforcement, chargeback, and smarter runtime routing (e.g., cheaper model variant fallback when cost SLO is breached).

Scope and Applicability:
- Applies to a...

title: PRD: Streaming-to-Batch Hybrid Runtime — API, SLOs, Pricing, and Rollout
preview:
Summary:
This PRD defines the product requirements for a Streaming-to-Batch Hybrid Runtime (\"hybrid runtime\") that unifies low-latency streaming and high-throughput batched inference in a single serving surface. The hybrid runtime should allow per-route policies that select streaming, batched, or hybrid execution,...
text:
Summary:
This PRD defines the product requirements for a Streaming-to-Batch Hybrid Runtime (\"hybrid runtime\") that unifies low-latency streaming and high-throughput batched inference in a single serving surface. The hybrid runtime should allow per-route policies that select streaming, batched, or hybrid execution, provide transparent pricing breakdowns, and preserve existing SLOs for customers during rollout.

Goals (what success looks like):
- Enable a single API surface where developers can request streaming, batched, or hybrid inference with minimal code change.
- Achieve median 50–150ms token latency for streaming routes under light load, and 2–4x cost reduction on high-concurrency transactional workloads via intelligent batching.
- Provide per-request cost and latency attribution in the Console and Billing API to make tradeoffs visible.
- Ship a staged rollout with canary, per-...

title: Field Operator Offline Debugging & Synthetic SLA Replay Playbook
preview:
Overview:

This playbook describes the standardized process for field operators and on-call engineers to perform offline debugging of private-deploy runtime incidents and to run deterministic synthetic SLA replay tests. Use this when a customer site experiences intermittent or reproducible SLA regressions but live t...
text:
Overview:

This playbook describes the standardized process for field operators and on-call engineers to perform offline debugging of private-deploy runtime incidents and to run deterministic synthetic SLA replay tests. Use this when a customer site experiences intermittent or reproducible SLA regressions but live traffic replay is not possible or desirable (air-gapped sites, PII constraints, or when reproducing production load would risk further degradation).

Goals:
- Produce a deterministic reproduction of the failing condition with minimal customer impact.
- Validate mitigation and rollout changes by running synthetic SLA replays against a controlled replica of the tenant environment.
- Capture forensics and telemetry artifacts required for post-incident review and compliance evidence.

When to use this playbook:
- Repeated elevated p95/p99 latency for inference endpoints with no ...

title: Assurance Convergence Protocol: Controls Alignment for Auth, Logging, Residency & SDLC
preview:
Summary
This document defines the Assurance Convergence Protocol (ACP): a single operational rubric that aligns RBAC and authentication controls, audit logging guarantees, data residency and retention schedules, secure SDLC gates, and incident-response evidence workflows. The ACP exists to reduce control gaps, accel...
text:
Summary
This document defines the Assurance Convergence Protocol (ACP): a single operational rubric that aligns RBAC and authentication controls, audit logging guarantees, data residency and retention schedules, secure SDLC gates, and incident-response evidence workflows. The ACP exists to reduce control gaps, accelerate audit preparation, and make exception and risk decisions traceable and reversible.

Scope
- Applies to hosted, dedicated, and private deployments (Redwood API, Dedicated capacity, and Private/VPC builds).
- Covers: role/permission lifecycle, authn/authz telemetry, audit-log schema & ingestion, retention/residency mapping for customer and telemetry data, SDLC release gates that require security evidence, and incident evidence capture & handoff.
- Excludes product feature design decisions; those are covered in product PRDs but must reference the ACP when they introduce ...

title: concurrency-primitives-and-affinity-guidelines-2026
preview:
Overview

This document defines recommended concurrency primitives, thread/actor affinity patterns, and safe integration points for Redwood's inference runtime and associated services. The goal is to reduce latency variability, avoid priority inversion on shared GPU/CPU resources, and make component-level behavior p...
text:
Overview

This document defines recommended concurrency primitives, thread/actor affinity patterns, and safe integration points for Redwood's inference runtime and associated services. The goal is to reduce latency variability, avoid priority inversion on shared GPU/CPU resources, and make component-level behavior predictable under both steady-state and burst loads.

Audience
- Runtime engineers
- Infra and SRE teams
- SDK and client-library maintainers

Goals
- Provide low-friction, testable concurrency patterns that limit tail-latency impact on multi-tenant GPU hosts.
- Standardize affinity approaches across CPU-bound pre/post processing, scheduler threads, and GPU work submission paths.
- Give clear migration guidance away from ad-hoc locking and blocking I/O in hot paths.

Scope
This guideline covers:
- Inference serving processes (request dispatch, KV cache, batching)
- Pre/post-...

title: Model Exfiltration: Evidence Capture, RBAC Containment, and Compliance Packaging
preview:
Overview:

This document defines the standard operating procedure for responding to suspected or confirmed model exfiltration incidents involving Redwood-hosted inference systems. It combines RBAC containment controls, audit log evidence capture queries, data-residency and retention considerations, secure-SDLC remed...
text:
Overview:

This document defines the standard operating procedure for responding to suspected or confirmed model exfiltration incidents involving Redwood-hosted inference systems. It combines RBAC containment controls, audit log evidence capture queries, data-residency and retention considerations, secure-SDLC remediation guidance, and the compliance packaging checklist required for regulatory/enterprise customers.

Scope and intent:
- Applies to all incidents where model internals, embeddings, prompt corpora, or customer-provided data may have been exfiltrated from any Redwood environment (hosted, dedicated, private).
- Focus is on rapid containment, defensible forensic evidence capture, and creating a reproducible compliance evidence package for audits.

Goals:
1. Stop ongoing exfiltration within 15 minutes of detection where possible.
2. Preserve forensic evidence with documented c...

title: Graceful Restart Latency Contracts and Snapshot Synthesis
preview:
Overview

This document defines latency contracts (SLOs/SLO targets) and the operational protocol for graceful restarts of runtime workers, kernel modules, and model shards. It also specifies the snapshot synthesis format we use for resumable execution: what state is captured, how it is validated, and how resume/rol...
text:
Overview

This document defines latency contracts (SLOs/SLO targets) and the operational protocol for graceful restarts of runtime workers, kernel modules, and model shards. It also specifies the snapshot synthesis format we use for resumable execution: what state is captured, how it is validated, and how resume/rollback works. The goal is to provide a concrete, testable, and observable procedure that keeps customer-facing latency and correctness within predictable bounds during maintenance, upgrades, and transient failures.

Audience

- Runtime engineers implementing restart semantics and kernel handoff.
- SRE and oncall responsible for incident response when restarts impact SLOs.
- Release engineers and QA writing canary tests and fidelity benchmarks.

Definitions

- graceful restart: a coordinated shutdown and resume of a worker (or kernel) that preserves sufficient execution state...

text:
Overview

Purpose: This playbook defines a compact, observable protocol for advertising runtime capabilities and negotiating effective behavior between clients and Redwood inference endpoints. The protocol reduces brittle client checks, enables safe feature rollouts, and provides structured signals for debugging and cost accounting.

Scope: Applies to HTTP and gRPC endpoints that expose evolving inference features (streaming modes, structured outputs, function/tool calling semantics, fidelity/quantization hints, early-exit heuristics). Exempt: purely documentation-only changes and telemetry-only fields.

Background and motivation
- Frequent client breakages occurred when runtime features changed without an explicit compatibility surface.
- Feature guards and ad-hoc query params proved inadequate for multi-variant deployments and private/legacy tenants.
- A negotiation layer allows cli...

title: SDK telemetry mismatch — canonical detection & fixes
preview:
Summary
=======

This document describes the common telemetry-mismatch patterns we see between Redwood client SDKs and the Redwood serving/control plane, concrete detection heuristics, triage steps, canonical fixes (SDK and server-side), and recommended tests to prevent regressions. The audience is SDK engineers, de...
text:
Summary
=======

This document describes the common telemetry-mismatch patterns we see between Redwood client SDKs and the Redwood serving/control plane, concrete detection heuristics, triage steps, canonical fixes (SDK and server-side), and recommended tests to prevent regressions. The audience is SDK engineers, developer-experience support, and oncall SREs.

Why this matters
------------------

Telemetry fidelity is required for accurate cost attribution, latency breakdowns, and automatic routing decisions. Mismatches lead to:
- Incorrect token counts in billing dashboards
- Misattributed latency or error spikes that obscure root cause
- Invalid sampling for analytics or A/B rollouts
- Failed dependability checks in canary deployments

Common symptom categories
-------------------------

1) Token count delta — SDK reports fewer/more tokens than server: typically caused by encoding d...

title: Adaptive Eval Funnel and Cost-Aware Sampling Playbook
preview:
Overview:

This playbook defines the Adaptive Eval Funnel (AEF) — a standardized, cost-aware sampling and prioritization pipeline for running continuous evaluation at scale. AEF is designed to reduce token and compute spend by 40–70% relative to naive full-scan evals while preserving coverage for high-risk slices an...
text:
Overview:

This playbook defines the Adaptive Eval Funnel (AEF) — a standardized, cost-aware sampling and prioritization pipeline for running continuous evaluation at scale. AEF is designed to reduce token and compute spend by 40–70% relative to naive full-scan evals while preserving coverage for high-risk slices and regression detection sensitivity.

Goals:
- Maintain high-fidelity regression detection on high-risk slices (precision >= 0.90 on targeted failure classes).
- Reduce end-to-end eval cost per release by 40% (measured in GPU-minutes and token units).
- Provide reproducible, auditable sampling decisions and a transparent budget allocation for product/tenant workloads.

Scope and audience:
- Scope: applies to model onboarding, nightly and pre-release eval runs, and production regression triage workload mirroring. Does NOT replace small-sample human evaluation for subjective q...

title: Shadow Traffic Synthesis and Acceptance Playbook
preview:
Summary
This playbook defines the standard process, tooling, and quantitative acceptance criteria for using shadow traffic (synthetic or mirrored) to validate model variants, serving runtime changes, and routing policies before progressive rollouts. Shadow traffic testing is required for all significant inference-si...
text:
Summary
This playbook defines the standard process, tooling, and quantitative acceptance criteria for using shadow traffic (synthetic or mirrored) to validate model variants, serving runtime changes, and routing policies before progressive rollouts. Shadow traffic testing is required for all significant inference-side changes that could affect latency, correctness, or cost (new model variants, quantization changes, runtime kernel upgrades, routing changes, and batching policy adjustments).

Scope and intent
- Scope: applies to hosted Redwood API, Dedicated pools, and Private runtime variants.
- Intent: catch regressions in latency, error behavior, output quality, resource utilization, and telemetry before exposing requests to production traffic.
- Not a replacement for production canaries; designed to be a high-fidelity, low-risk verification step.

Roles and ownership
1. Change owner...

title: Mini Emulator + Telemetry Mirror Playbook for SDK Onboarding
preview:
Summary

This playbook explains the 'mini emulator' pattern we use for rapid SDK onboarding, local integration tests, and Support first-response debugging. The emulator is a lightweight, single-process stub that reproduces key runtime behaviors of Redwood's hosted inference API: streaming, batching (approximate beha...
text:
Summary

This playbook explains the 'mini emulator' pattern we use for rapid SDK onboarding, local integration tests, and Support first-response debugging. The emulator is a lightweight, single-process stub that reproduces key runtime behaviors of Redwood's hosted inference API: streaming, batching (approximate behavior), token accounting, configurable latency, and an optional telemetry mirror that emits a compact golden trace for deterministic replay. The goal is fast developer feedback loops and a reproducible first-level-support workflow.

Intended audience

- SDK engineers authoring client libraries
- Developer Experience (DevX) engineers creating quickstarts and templates
- Support engineers doing rapid reproduction and canonical fixes

Motivation

New integrators often get stuck on three recurring issues: (1) streaming/diffing mismatches, (2) token-count and pricing surprises, a...

title: SLO Safeguard & Canary Governance Playbook
preview:
Overview:
This playbook defines governance, operational controls, and tooling patterns for running SLO-focused experiments and model canaries on Redwood's inference fleet. It establishes a safety-first approach so teams can iterate model rollouts and runtime tuning while protecting customer-facing SLOs and error bud...
text:
Overview:
This playbook defines governance, operational controls, and tooling patterns for running SLO-focused experiments and model canaries on Redwood's inference fleet. It establishes a safety-first approach so teams can iterate model rollouts and runtime tuning while protecting customer-facing SLOs and error budgets.

Scope:
- Applies to any production-facing model rollout, quantization change, kernel-switch, or runtime configuration that can alter latency, tail latency, or correctness metrics.
- Intended audience: SREs, Service Stewards, Model Owners, Capacity Engineers, Release Engineers.

Definitions:
- SLO (Service Level Objective): the target level of reliability (example: p99 latency & availability).
- Error Budget: allowable SLO violation over a window (30d default).
- Canary: a staged rollout (traffic percentage + time) used to validate changes.
- Safety Net: automated and...

title: Promotion Readiness: Cross-Stage Synthetic Simulation & Quantization Safety Playbook
preview:
Summary

This playbook defines a combined evaluation and safety protocol used to decide when a model artifact can be promoted from evaluation (canary/validation) into production routing. It coordinates three corridors of checks: 1) synthetic user simulation to validate behavioral and distributional stability, 2) qua...
text:
Summary

This playbook defines a combined evaluation and safety protocol used to decide when a model artifact can be promoted from evaluation (canary/validation) into production routing. It coordinates three corridors of checks: 1) synthetic user simulation to validate behavioral and distributional stability, 2) quantization & kernel-sensitivity safety checks for performance-preserving transforms, and 3) lightweight runtime smoke and canary procedures to exercise operational invariants. The goal is deterministic, reproducible promotion decisions tied to explicit pass/fail signals and rollback criteria.

Scope and Use Cases

- Applies to any model promotion that will be served by Redwood runtime variants where quantized or variant kernels (FP16/INT8/QLoRA-style) are in the promotion path.
- Intended for models with expected production throughput above 1 qps/customer or with regulatory/...

title: Execution Observer and Fallback Verification Manual
preview:
Overview

text:
Overview

Purpose: This manual describes the Execution Observer subsystem and the verification procedures for the runtime's fallback orchestration. The Execution Observer (exec-observer) is the deterministic diagnostic and verification component used in CI, canary rollouts, and incident replay to validate that fallback decisions preserve SLOs and maintain state correctness.

Scope: architecture, decision telemetry, verification harnesses, profiling scenarios, benchmarking profiles, runbook steps for validation and rollback, ownership, and acceptance criteria for changes to fallback policies and observer instrumentation.

Audience: runtime engineers, SREs, release engineers, and oncall responders.

1) System summary

What exec-observer is:
- A light-weight sidecar-style observer process that subscribes to the routing/decision events emitted by the runtime decision fabric.
- Responsible...

title: Postmortem: SEV-2 Console rollout UI bug triggered rollback loop (2026-02-22)

text: A Console UI change caused repeated client-side retries on a non-idempotent rollback endpoint, putting rollouts-service into a rollback loop for a subset of deployments.

title: Status timeline automation spec and template library
preview:
Overview

This page is the canonical library for: (A) structured incident status timelines used by Redwood Inference responder teams and (B) the automation specification for generating and publishing those timelines to the status page, internal Slack bridge, and customer notification channels. The goal is fast, cons...
text:
Overview

This page is the canonical library for: (A) structured incident status timelines used by Redwood Inference responder teams and (B) the automation specification for generating and publishing those timelines to the status page, internal Slack bridge, and customer notification channels. The goal is fast, consistent, auditable customer communications that map to incident phases and operator actions.

Who should use this

- On-call SREs and service owners who publish incident updates
- Incident bridge operators and communications owners
- Automation engineers who implement status page webhooks and templates
- Customer Success for pre-approved messaging when high-impact incidents occur

Principles

1) Single source of truth: the status timeline generator (STG) must accept a single incident payload (incident_id) and emit consistent updates to all channels.
2) Phase-first messaging:...

title: Eval cost slice prioritization and resource budgeting
preview:
Summary

This document defines a prioritization and budgeting framework for running slice-driven evaluations in Redwood’s eval harness. It describes a deterministic scoring function for slice prioritization, recommended resource budgets for common evaluation classes (smoke, micro-canary, full-slice, long-tail scan),...
text:
Summary

This document defines a prioritization and budgeting framework for running slice-driven evaluations in Redwood’s eval harness. It describes a deterministic scoring function for slice prioritization, recommended resource budgets for common evaluation classes (smoke, micro-canary, full-slice, long-tail scan), integration points with quantization profiles and onboarding, and a triage fast-path for regression detection and mitigation.

Why this exists

- Eval budgets are finite: the eval harness runs thousands of slices across many models and releases. Uncontrolled execution causes high cost and slow feedback.
- We need consistent signal for promotion and regression triage while minimizing GPU/CPU spend.
- Quantization changes and heterogeneous deployments (private/dedicated) require different resource profiles; this doc standardizes budgeting and prioritization so teams follow p...

title: Customer Success Observability Onboarding Kit
preview:
Overview:
This kit documents the recommended observability and telemetry configuration, validation tests, and post-cutover monitoring runbook that Customer Success and Platform Engineering use to hand off an enterprise customer at go‑live. The goal is to ensure we can reliably measure the customer’s production healt...
text:
Overview:
This kit documents the recommended observability and telemetry configuration, validation tests, and post-cutover monitoring runbook that Customer Success and Platform Engineering use to hand off an enterprise customer at go‑live. The goal is to ensure we can reliably measure the customer’s production health within 10 minutes of cutover for the key inference and cost SLOs, and to provide clear stop/rollback criteria if signals indicate instability.

Scope:
- Applies to enterprise Dedicated and Private deployments where Redwood operates a telemetry gatekeeper or where the customer forwards metrics/traces/logs into our observability pipeline.
- Excludes ad-hoc POCs and single-request evaluation accounts.

Audience:
- Customer Success Onboarding PMs
- Platform/Infra Engineers
- Solutions Engineers and SREs

Key outcomes after following this kit:
1) Instrumentation baseline is va...

title: VPC Handshake and Operator Blueprint for Private Deploys
preview:
Summary:
This playbook defines the VPC "handshake" process and an operator blueprint to safely install, validate, and operate Redwood Private deployments. It combines deployment handoff steps, on-prem packaging checks, day-2 operational guardrails, and a deterministic upgrade/rollback procedure intended for field op...
text:
Summary:
This playbook defines the VPC "handshake" process and an operator blueprint to safely install, validate, and operate Redwood Private deployments. It combines deployment handoff steps, on-prem packaging checks, day-2 operational guardrails, and a deterministic upgrade/rollback procedure intended for field operators and delivery engineers.

Goals:
- Standardize handoff between Delivery/Install and Customer Ops.
- Ensure a signed, verifiable package and immutable boot path for air-gapped and VPC installs.
- Provide a concise day-2 operator playbook for common incidents, model swaps, and upgrades.
- Define customer-facing requirements and acceptance tests required to proceed to production.

Owners and contact:
- Primary owner: eng-private-deployments field-integration (pager: @field-oncall)
- Secondary owner: eng-platform - runtime (email: runtime-ops@redwood.ai)
- Delivery conta...

title: Customer network, firmware, and KMS readiness checklist for private deployments
preview:
Summary:
This checklist captures the minimum network, firmware/driver, and KMS readiness items we require from customers before we begin an on-prem / VPC private deployment handoff. The goal is to reduce field friction during installation, ensure predictable runtime behavior for the Redwood inference runtime, and li...
text:
Summary:
This checklist captures the minimum network, firmware/driver, and KMS readiness items we require from customers before we begin an on-prem / VPC private deployment handoff. The goal is to reduce field friction during installation, ensure predictable runtime behavior for the Redwood inference runtime, and limit post-installation remedial work. Use this checklist during pre-sales technical validation and include a signed acceptance by the customer prior to scheduling the delivery window.

Audience:
- Solutions engineers and field systems integrators
- Customer infrastructure and security teams
- Private deployment delivery engineers and SREs

Key outcomes:
- Confirmed network paths and firewall rules for control plane, model serving, and telemetry
- Minimum validated firmware and driver versions across GPU hosts and networking gear
- KMS/HSM integration plan with rotation sched...

title: Holiday & Maintenance Window On-call Orchestration and Customer Communication Toolkit
preview:
Overview

Purpose: This document standardizes how Redwood operates scheduled maintenance and holiday windows when staffing is reduced. It covers on-call rotation adaptations, a concise operational runbook for scheduled degradations, pre- and post-maintenance game-day exercises, and customer-facing status templates. ...
text:
Overview

Purpose: This document standardizes how Redwood operates scheduled maintenance and holiday windows when staffing is reduced. It covers on-call rotation adaptations, a concise operational runbook for scheduled degradations, pre- and post-maintenance game-day exercises, and customer-facing status templates. The goal is to maintain SLOs where possible, minimize customer surprise, and ensure a clear, timeboxed recovery path if regressions occur.

Scope
- Services in-scope: redwood-serving, routing-proxy, kv-cache, console-api, billing-queue, telemetry-ingest.
- Applies to: company-approved maintenance (deploys, infra upgrades, region maintenance) and planned holiday staffing reductions (global public holidays, end-of-year window).

Key principles
- Prioritize availability-critical routes: routing-proxy and redwood-serving production routes must be treated with highest priority.

title: Adaptive Prefetch Hint Layer: design, operator guide, and rollout
preview:
Summary
-------
This document describes the Adaptive Prefetch Hint Layer (APHL): a lightweight application-facing hinting protocol and runtime integration that lets upstream services provide controlled prefetch requests to the LLM runtime to reduce tail latency for common prefixes while preserving global SLOs and co...
text:
Summary
-------
This document describes the Adaptive Prefetch Hint Layer (APHL): a lightweight application-facing hinting protocol and runtime integration that lets upstream services provide controlled prefetch requests to the LLM runtime to reduce tail latency for common prefixes while preserving global SLOs and cost budgets. It includes design rationale, runtime components, operator playbooks, dashboarding requirements, and a safe rollout plan.

Intended audience
- Platform operators and on-call engineers
- Runtime and serving engineers
- Client SDK and API owners

Why we built APHL
- Observation: a small fraction of request patterns (e.g., prompt templates, system messages, high-frequency webhook patterns) dominate 75% of tail latency because early tokens are not hot in the prefix/KV cache.
- Goal: reduce p95/p99 generation latency for known safe prefixes without increasing token c...

title: Policy-driven streaming and early-exit contract
preview:
Summary
-------
This document defines a standardized API contract and runtime expectations for policy-driven streaming generation endpoints, with explicit support for safe early-exit, client-driven backpressure, and per-request cost/quality hints. The goal is to make streaming behavior predictable across hosted, ded...
text:
Summary
-------
This document defines a standardized API contract and runtime expectations for policy-driven streaming generation endpoints, with explicit support for safe early-exit, client-driven backpressure, and per-request cost/quality hints. The goal is to make streaming behavior predictable across hosted, dedicated, and private deployments while enabling cost-aware clients and graceful degradation strategies.

Scope
-----
Applies to all Redwood inference endpoints that offer streaming token/response delivery (hosted API and dedicated model variants). This contract covers: request schema additions, streaming frame types, control frames (early-exit, watermark, throttling), header conventions, server-side metrics, compatibility rules, and rollout/testing procedures.

Motivation
----------
- Many client integrations require deterministic signal semantics for when to stop rendering ...

# Summary
Console preview is now powered by the control plane simulation endpoint and we are back on track for the beta cohort kickoff.

# Risks / blockers
- Multi-region simulation remains unscoped; could be requested by beta customers.

# Next week

- Publish beta onboarding doc and track adoption metrics.

title: Config Consistency and Gated Feature Flags Playbook
preview:
Overview:
This playbook defines our standard architecture and operational practices for distributing runtime configuration (feature flags, quantization/precision profiles, autoscaling hints, and routing policies) with a focus on strong eventual consistency, safe progressive rollouts, and automated SLI/SLO safety gat...
text:
Overview:
This playbook defines our standard architecture and operational practices for distributing runtime configuration (feature flags, quantization/precision profiles, autoscaling hints, and routing policies) with a focus on strong eventual consistency, safe progressive rollouts, and automated SLI/SLO safety gates. It is intended for platform engineers, runtime teams, and service owners who operate multi-tenant inference services (Hosted, Dedicated, Private).

Problem statement:
- Runtime configuration changes (flags, model variants, quantization profiles) are frequent and can change behavior across thousands of nodes instantly.
- Inconsistencies between control-plane config and node application config cause correctness, performance regressions, and hard-to-debug incidents.
- Feature-flag-driven rollouts (canary, ring, percentage rollouts) must be reversible with bounded blast rad...

title: Adaptive KV Coordinator and Eviction Contract
preview:
Overview

This document specifies the runtime contract for the Adaptive KV Coordinator (AKC), the eviction policy interface, and the safety guarantees that downstream services (shard-manager, runtime-executor, request-router) rely on. It is intended for runtime engineers, infra SREs, model-serving owners, and privat...
text:
Overview

This document specifies the runtime contract for the Adaptive KV Coordinator (AKC), the eviction policy interface, and the safety guarantees that downstream services (shard-manager, runtime-executor, request-router) rely on. It is intended for runtime engineers, infra SREs, model-serving owners, and private deployment integrators.

Goals

- Define a minimal, implementation-agnostic API for KV cache coordination across shards and variants.
- Provide deterministic eviction semantics under constrained memory pressure to avoid cross-variant nondeterminism.
- Specify telemetry, SLOs, and runtime knobs required for safe rollouts and private deployments.
- Outline a migration path and rollback plan for changing eviction strategies.

Scope and non-goals

- Scope: in-memory KV caches used for model KV caches (attention cache, prefix cache) and request-level prefix caches managed by r...

title: Responder wellbeing and orchestration guide
preview:
Overview

Purpose: This guide unifies three practical priorities for Redwood responders: (1) a compact incident lifecycle focused on fast containment and humane workload, (2) repeatable runbook patterns and postmortem scaffolding, and (3) on-call rotation and game-day design that preserves responder wellbeing while ...
text:
Overview

Purpose: This guide unifies three practical priorities for Redwood responders: (1) a compact incident lifecycle focused on fast containment and humane workload, (2) repeatable runbook patterns and postmortem scaffolding, and (3) on-call rotation and game-day design that preserves responder wellbeing while meeting SLO obligations.

Scope: applies to all teams that operate inference services (api-frontend, inference-router, kv-cache, model-host). This is an operational reference — for team-specific runbooks link to the service page.

Owner and escalation

- Primary owner: Eng SRE (pager rotation managed in OnCall roster).
- Secondary owner: team that owns the impacted service (see escalation matrix below).
- Pager escalation: primary -> secondary -> SRE lead -> CTO on extended incidents (>4 hours).

Escalation matrix (quick view)

| Symptom | Primary | Secondary | Escalate aft...

title: Persona Bias Probing and Anchor Eval Framework
preview:
Overview

This document defines the Persona Bias Probing and Anchor Eval Framework (PBAEF), an evaluation harness designed to: 1) detect persona-conditional bias and safety regressions, 2) provide stable anchor baselines for longitudinal calibration, and 3) integrate with Redwood’s eval orchestration for automated t...
text:
Overview

This document defines the Persona Bias Probing and Anchor Eval Framework (PBAEF), an evaluation harness designed to: 1) detect persona-conditional bias and safety regressions, 2) provide stable anchor baselines for longitudinal calibration, and 3) integrate with Redwood’s eval orchestration for automated triage and alerting. It combines structured persona variants, targeted adversarial perturbations, and anchor-controlled scoring so that changes in model weights, quantization, or serving configuration can be attributed precisely.

Goals and scope

- Primary goals:
 - Surface persona-dependent failure modes (bias, toxicity, hallucination) across representative workloads.
 - Provide anchor baselines that remain stable across model promotions and deployments to detect relative regressions.
 - Integrate with regression triage playbooks to shorten mean time to detect and resolve ...

title: SLO Impact Simulator and Capacity Sandbox Playbook
preview:
Summary
-------
This playbook documents the SLO Impact Simulator and Capacity Sandbox ("SISCS"), a reproducible environment and procedure for validating SLO changes, error-budget policies, autoscaling rules, and fallback strategies before applying them to production. SISCS is a low-risk way to estimate user-facing a...
text:
Summary
-------
This playbook documents the SLO Impact Simulator and Capacity Sandbox ("SISCS"), a reproducible environment and procedure for validating SLO changes, error-budget policies, autoscaling rules, and fallback strategies before applying them to production. SISCS is a low-risk way to estimate user-facing availability, latency, and cost impacts across traffic patterns and capacity tiers.

Goals
-----
- Provide an executable sandbox to simulate traffic, faults, and routing policies against a production-like stack.
- Quantify SLO and error budget outcomes for proposed changes (SLO tightening, burst handling, reduced reserved capacity).
- Surface regressions in observability, alerting, and oncall playbooks before rollout.
- Produce reproducible experiment artifacts and a clear decision log for stakeholders.

Scope and non-goals
-------------------
- In-scope: model-serving stack...

title: Runtime concurrency isolation and telemetry handbook
preview:
Overview
========
This handbook describes Redwood Inference's conventions and operational practices for runtime concurrency isolation, lane-based request shaping, and the minimum telemetry required to detect and diagnose cross-tenant or cross-model interference. It consolidates architecture rationale, configuration ...
text:
Overview
========
This handbook describes Redwood Inference's conventions and operational practices for runtime concurrency isolation, lane-based request shaping, and the minimum telemetry required to detect and diagnose cross-tenant or cross-model interference. It consolidates architecture rationale, configuration examples, diagnostics runbooks, and rollout guidance for changing isolation or sampling policies in production.

Goals
-----
- Ensure noisy tenants or long-context workloads do not violate p95/p99 latency SLOs for other tenants sharing GPUs.
- Provide a consistent taxonomy (lanes, credits, isolation zones) for engineers and SREs to reason about scheduling and fairness.
- Define telemetry, sampling, and alerting so incidents can be triaged without deep kernel-level tracing for every case.

Scope
-----
This doc covers: request lanes, concurrency caps, credit accounting surfac...

title: Platform sidecar contracts and healthcheck standards
preview:
Summary

Purpose: Define a concise, enforceable contract for platform sidecars so that every service running on Redwood’s platform has consistent health checks, metrics, lifecycle behavior, and alerting. This reduces incidents caused by inconsistent sidecar implementations and speeds onboarding of new sidecars.

Sco...
text:
Summary

Purpose: Define a concise, enforceable contract for platform sidecars so that every service running on Redwood’s platform has consistent health checks, metrics, lifecycle behavior, and alerting. This reduces incidents caused by inconsistent sidecar implementations and speeds onboarding of new sidecars.

Scope: Applies to all new platform-managed sidecars (network proxies, auth-injectors, telemetry-forwarders, cache-warmers) deployed to Redwood clusters (hosted and Dedicated). Does NOT replace application-level probes; it standardizes sidecar behavior only.

Key goals
- Deterministic health endpoints and semantics (liveness, readiness, readiness gating).
- Minimum metric set and Prometheus labels for correlation.
- Standardized Kubernetes probe specs and resource budgets.
- Dashboard and alert templates for immediate on-call visibility.
- Clear ownership and onboarding/runbook...

title: SLO Contract Exchange Protocol for Multi-Tenant Bursting (SCEP)
preview:
Summary
-------
This document defines the SLO Contract Exchange Protocol (SCEP): a lightweight, machine-readable contract and operational workflow that governs temporary capacity borrowing (bursting) between tenant services and the platform during demand spikes. SCEP aligns SLOs, error budgets, and capacity reconcil...
text:
Summary
-------
This document defines the SLO Contract Exchange Protocol (SCEP): a lightweight, machine-readable contract and operational workflow that governs temporary capacity borrowing (bursting) between tenant services and the platform during demand spikes. SCEP aligns SLOs, error budgets, and capacity reconciliation to allow predictable short-term overprovisioning while protecting platform-wide reliability.

Scope
-----
- Applies to all multi-tenant inference workloads served by Redwood’s hosted and dedicated lanes.
- Covers burst events where a tenant requests >20% additional token-serving capacity for up to 72 hours.
- Does not replace long-term capacity reservations or formal Dedicated contracts.

Goals
-----
1) Provide a standard schema for temporary capacity requests tied to explicit SLO concessions.
2) Automate policy evaluation and capacity allocation where possible.
3) R...

# Summary
We hit a console delivery risk: validation and policy preview work is larger than planned, and the beta cohort onboarding may slip.

# Status
- Overall: At Risk
- RAG: Yellow

# Progress this week
- Console policy editor: added compatibility validation for model variants (client-side).
- Added basic policy preview panel showing expected canary split and fallback precedence.

# Risks / blockers
- Policy preview requires backend simulation endpoints; current implementation is static and may mislead users.
- Beta cohort (3 design partners) is waiting on a working preview to sign off.

# Mitigations
- Scope preview to a read-only backend call for a single route and a single region first.
- Provide a temporary “preview is approximate” disclaimer until simulation is complete.

title: Ops conductor patterns and scenario worksheets
preview:
Summary:

This document defines a set of repeatable incident-command patterns (the Ops Conductor patterns) and scenario worksheets for on-call teams owning LLM inference services. It complements line-item runbooks by codifying the orchestration roles, step sequences, and customer communication templates for common a...
text:
Summary:

This document defines a set of repeatable incident-command patterns (the Ops Conductor patterns) and scenario worksheets for on-call teams owning LLM inference services. It complements line-item runbooks by codifying the orchestration roles, step sequences, and customer communication templates for common and high‑impact events (capacity spikes, KV cache thrashing, model quality regressions, and regional failovers).

Scope:
- Applies to all on-call responders for inference services: inference-gateway, route-router, kv-cache, model-hostpool, console-api, and billing-proxy.
- Intended for use during live incidents, game-day rehearsals, and postmortem evidence gathering.

Intent and benefits:
- Reduce cognitive load on first responders by prescribing clear conductor/bridge patterns.
- Shorten mean time to acknowledgement (MTA) and mean time to repair (MTTR) via pre-baked decisio...
